examples.title: Cloud Deployment Model Simulator
examples.description: Demonstrates the fundamental differences between Public, Private, Hybrid, and Community Cloud deployment models through a simulated cloud management system
examples.code:
class CloudDeployment:
    def __init__(self, name, deployment_type):
        self.name = name
        self.deployment_type = deployment_type
        self.resources = {}
    
    def allocate_resource(self, resource_type, amount):
        self.resources[resource_type] = amount
        return f"Allocated {amount} {resource_type} to {self.name} ({self.deployment_type})"

# Public Cloud - Multi-tenant, owned by cloud service provider
public_cloud = CloudDeployment("AWS EC2", "Public Cloud")
print("=== PUBLIC CLOUD ===")
print(public_cloud.allocate_resource("Virtual Machines", 10))
print(public_cloud.allocate_resource("Storage (GB)", 500))
print("Characteristics: Shared infrastructure, pay-per-use, multi-tenant")

# Private Cloud - Single organization, dedicated infrastructure
private_cloud = CloudDeployment("VMware Private Cloud", "Private Cloud")
print("\n=== PRIVATE CLOUD ===")
print(private_cloud.allocate_resource("Virtual Machines", 50))
print(private_cloud.allocate_resource("Storage (TB)", 10))
print("Characteristics: Dedicated infrastructure, enhanced security, full control")

# Hybrid Cloud - Combines public and private
hybrid_cloud = CloudDeployment("Azure Hybrid", "Hybrid Cloud")
print("\n=== HYBRID CLOUD ===")
print(hybrid_cloud.allocate_resource("Public VMs", 20))
print(hybrid_cloud.allocate_resource("Private VMs", 30))
print("Characteristics: Workload portability, burst capacity, disaster recovery")

# Community Cloud - Shared by multiple organizations
community_cloud = CloudDeployment("Government Cloud", "Community Cloud")
print("\n=== COMMUNITY CLOUD ===")
print(community_cloud.allocate_resource("Shared VMs", 100))
print(community_cloud.allocate_resource("Secure Storage", 1000))
print("Characteristics: Shared by specific community, compliance-focused")
examples.explanation: This code creates a class to simulate different cloud deployment models. Each deployment type shows typical characteristics: Public Cloud uses shared multi-tenant infrastructure, Private Cloud uses dedicated resources, Hybrid Cloud combines both, and Community Cloud is shared by organizations with common requirements.

examples.title: Workload Distribution in Hybrid Cloud
examples.description: Demonstrates how a hybrid cloud architecture distributes workloads between private and public clouds based on capacity and requirements
examples.code:
import time

class HybridCloudOrchestrator:
    def __init__(self):
        self.private_capacity = 1000  # units
        self.public_capacity = 500    # units
        self.private_used = 0
        self.public_used = 0
    
    def deploy_workload(self, workload_name, required_units):
        """Deploy workload to appropriate cloud based on capacity"""
        print(f"\nDeploying '{workload_name}' requiring {required_units} units...")
        
        # Check if private cloud can handle it
        available_private = self.private_capacity - self.private_used
        
        if required_units <= available_private:
            # Deploy to private cloud (sensitive/critical workloads)
            self.private_used += required_units
            print(f"  -> Deployed to PRIVATE CLOUD")
            print(f"  -> Private Cloud: {self.private_used}/{self.private_capacity} used")
            return "private"
        else:
            # Burst to public cloud (elastic workloads)
            remaining = required_units - available_private
            if remaining <= self.public_capacity:
                self.private_used = self.private_capacity  # Max out private
                self.public_used += remaining
                print(f"  -> Hybrid deployment: {available_private} private + {remaining} public")
                print(f"  -> Private Cloud: {self.private_used}/{self.private_capacity} used")
                print(f"  -> Public Cloud: {self.public_used}/{self.public_capacity} used")
                return "hybrid"
            else:
                print(f"  -> ERROR: Insufficient capacity!")
                return "failed"

# Simulate hybrid cloud workload distribution
orchestrator = HybridCloudOrchestrator()

# Deploy different types of workloads
workloads = [
    ("Critical Database", 300),        # Sensitive - goes to private
    ("Web Application", 400),           # Standard - goes to private
    ("Analytics Processing", 600),    # Burst - hybrid deployment
    ("Backup Service", 300),           # Burst - hybrid (now full)
    ("Machine Learning Training", 200)  # Overflow - to public only
]

print("=== HYBRID CLOUD WORKLOAD DISTRIBUTION ===")
for workload_name, units in workloads:
    result = orchestrator.deploy_workload(workload_name, units)
    time.sleep(0.5)  # Simulate deployment time

print("\n=== Summary ===")
print(f"Private Cloud Utilization: {orchestrator.private_used}/{orchestrator.private_capacity}")
print(f"Public Cloud Utilization: {orchestrator.public_used}/{orchestrator.public_capacity}")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates a hybrid cloud orchestrator that intelligently distributes workloads. Critical workloads deploy to private cloud for security, while demand spikes burst to public cloud. The code shows capacity management, workload placement decisions, and resource utilization tracking across both cloud environments.

examples.title: Multi-Cloud Resource Management with API Abstraction
examples.description: Advanced example showing abstraction layer for managing resources across Public, Private, and Community clouds with unified API
examples.explanation: This advanced example implements the Strategy and Factory design patterns to create a unified cloud management abstraction. It demonstrates: 1) Abstract base class for different cloud types 2) Concrete implementations for Public, Private, and Community clouds 3) A multi-cloud manager that abstracts away provider differences 4) Cost comparison across deployment models. This represents real-world multi-cloud strategies where organizations use different clouds for different purposes.
